Instead of fundamentally altering the model architecture for each modality, the approach is to develop a "tokenizer" for each data type. This converts the input, whatever it may be, into a sequence of token-like representations that the Transformer can process uniformly.

Early work like the VQGAN paper, "Taming Transformers for High-Resolution Image Synthesis," demonstrated how an image could be "tokenized." A CNN encoder processes the image and a quantization step maps patches of the image to a discrete set of integer codes from a learned codebook. This turns a continuous image into a 2D grid of integer "tokens," which can then be flattened into a 1D sequence for an autoregressive Transformer. More recently, OpenAI's video generation model, Sora, adopted a similar philosophy, as described in their technical report, "Video generation models as world simulators." They state, "Whereas LLMs have text tokens, Sora has visual patches." These visual patches are the token-equivalents for video data, created by first compressing the video into a lower-dimensional latent space. This unified representation allows a single Transformer model to be trained on visual data of varying durations, resolutions, and aspect ratios, just as an LLM handles text of varying lengths.

Revisiting Tokenization Issues and Final Thoughts — 1:52:39

Many of the most counter-intuitive behaviors of Large Language Models can be traced back to tokenization. For example, when GPT-4 is asked, "How many letters 'l' are there in the word 'DefaultCellStyle'?", it incorrectly answers three. The correct answer is four, but the model fails because the cl100k_base tokenizer encodes the entire string "DefaultCellStyle" as a single, indivisible token (ID 98518). The model never sees the individual letters, so it cannot count them. Similarly, asking the model to reverse the string results in jumbled output. However, if we rephrase the prompt to first break the word into characters separated by spaces (D e f a u l t...), the model succeeds. This is because the spaces force each character to become its own token, transforming a difficult character-level task into a simple token-reversal task that the model can easily perform.

This token-based view of the world also explains why models are often less proficient in non-English languages. The English phrase "Hello how are you?" is encoded into just 5 tokens, while its Korean translation, "안녕하세요 어떻게 지내세요?", requires 15 tokens—a 3x blow-up in length. This inefficiency reduces the model's effective context window and processing capability for that language. A similar issue plagues arithmetic. The standard algorithm for addition is performed digit-by-digit, but numbers are often tokenized into arbitrary chunks. A blog post titled "Integer tokenization is insane" highlights that the GPT-2 tokenizer assigns unique tokens to integers up to 521, but then represents larger numbers as inconsistent combinations of smaller tokens. This prevents the model from learning a generalizable algorithm for math. Recognizing this, developers of Meta's Llama 2, which uses the SentencePiece tokenizer, deliberately configured it to split all numbers into individual digit tokens, significantly improving its arithmetic abilities.

The way tokenization handles common patterns like whitespace can also push a model into "out-of-distribution" states, degrading its performance. When using a completion model like gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct in the OpenAI Playground, a prompt ending with a trailing space triggers a warning: "Your text ends in a trailing space, which causes worse performance..." This occurs because the trailing space is encoded as its own token (ID 220 in cl100k_base). In the model's training data, a space is almost always a prefix that is merged with the following word (e.g., the token for " Oh" includes the leading space). By providing a lone space at the end of a prompt, you create a token sequence that the model has rarely, if ever, seen during training. This forces it to predict the next token from an unfamiliar context, which can lead to poor or unpredictable results.

In some cases, specific tokens can act as triggers that cause models to fail in bizarre ways. When asked to simply print the string <|endoftext|>, ChatGPT-4 becomes confused and fails to produce the output. It interprets the string not as literal text but as a special control token that signals the end of a document. This creates a potential security vulnerability, as it shows that specially crafted user input can be processed as a command. This phenomenon of "weird tokens" is well-documented, most famously in the "SolidGoldMagikarp" case. Researchers found that certain tokens, which were present in the tokenizer's training data (e.g., from Reddit usernames) but absent from the model's own training corpus, caused the model to "go haywire." Because the model has no training data for these tokens, their embeddings are effectively random, and prompting with them can lead to a range of undefined behaviors, including evasion, hallucination, and even insults.
